title: How the body develops temperature dysregulation from trauma
author: Kalesh
excerpt: You're freezing when everyone else is sweating. Or you're drenched in sweat while they're reaching for a sweater. Your hands and feet are ice cold even in summ...
metaDescription: You're freezing when everyone else is sweating. Or you're drenched in sweat while they're reaching for a sweater. Your hands and feet are ice cold even in...
htmlContent:
You're freezing when everyone else is sweating. Or you're drenched in sweat while they're reaching for a sweater. Your hands and feet are ice cold even in summer. Your internal thermostat feels broken, like someone rewired it wrong. And you've probably been told it's "anxiety" or "hormones" or "you just need to drink more water."

But here's the thing. Your body isn't broken. It's making perfect sense. The temperature dysregulation you're experiencing is a direct, logical consequence of a nervous system that has been shaped by trauma. And understanding why this happens is the first step toward feeling safe in your own skin again.

The nervous system is your internal thermostat

Your body temperature isn't just controlled by the hypothalamus in your brain. It's deeply connected to your autonomic nervous system, specifically the two branches that Stephen Porges has spent decades mapping out. Porges, the neuroscientist who developed polyvagal theory, showed us that our nervous system has three distinct states: ventral vagal (safe and social), sympathetic (fight or flight), and dorsal vagal (shutdown or freeze).

Each of these states has a specific relationship with body temperature. When you're in ventral vagal, your body temperature regulates normally. Blood flows to your extremities. Your hands and feet are warm. You can sweat appropriately when it's hot and conserve heat when it's cold. Your system is flexible, responsive, adaptive.

But trauma changes this. When you've experienced chronic stress or overwhelming events, your nervous system gets stuck. It loses its ability to move fluidly between states. And this directly impacts how your body manages temperature.

Think about it. When you're in sympathetic activation, your body is preparing for danger. Blood rushes to your large muscles, away from your extremities. Your hands get cold. Your feet get cold. Your body is prioritizing survival over comfort. It doesn't care if your fingers are freezing. It's trying to keep you alive.

When you're in dorsal vagal shutdown, your body does something different. It conserves energy. Your metabolism slows. Your blood pressure drops. Your body temperature can actually decrease because your system is putting itself into hibernation mode. This is why some people with complex trauma report feeling cold all the time, even in warm environments.

And here's where it gets really interesting. You can flip between these states rapidly, which is why your temperature can swing wildly. One moment you're hot, the next you're cold. Your nervous system is ping-ponging between activation and shutdown, and your body temperature is along for the ride. A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link). It gives you a framework for understanding these states and how to work with them.

Why your hands and feet are always cold

This is one of the most common complaints I hear from trauma survivors. "My hands are freezing. My feet are like ice blocks. No matter what I do, they won't warm up." And they've tried everything. Socks, gloves, warm baths, space heaters. Nothing works for long.

The reason is simple but . Your blood vessels are constricting in response to a nervous system that perceives threat. This is called vasoconstriction, and it's controlled by your sympathetic nervous system. When you're in a chronic state of hypervigilance, your body keeps the blood away from your extremities because it assumes danger is imminent.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote The Body Keeps the Score, has spent his career showing us that trauma lives in the body. It's not just a memory in your mind. It's a physiological reality. Your body remembers the threat even when your conscious mind knows you're safe. And it keeps responding accordingly.

So your cold hands aren't a circulation problem. They're a safety problem. Your nervous system is saying, "I don't feel safe enough to let blood flow to my fingers. I need to keep it near my core in case I need to run or fight."

This is why no amount of external warmth fully resolves the issue. You can put your hands in warm water and they'll warm up temporarily, but the moment you take them out, they go cold again. Because the internal signal hasn't changed. Your nervous system is still sending the same message.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Van der Kolk walks through the science of how trauma reshapes the body, including temperature regulation, and offers pathways toward healing.

The overheating response and trauma

But not everyone with trauma runs cold. Some people run hot. They're always sweating, always feeling flushed, always too warm. And this makes just as much sense from a nervous system perspective.

When your sympathetic nervous system is chronically activated, your body is in a constant state of metabolic arousal. Your heart rate is elevated. Your breathing is shallow. Your muscles are tense. And all of this generates heat. Your body is running a low-grade fever because it's preparing for action that never comes.

This is why some trauma survivors experience hot flashes or night sweats that aren't related to hormones. Their nervous system is dumping heat because it's in a constant state of readiness. The body is trying to cool itself down from an internal fire that won't go out.

And then there's the phenomenon of emotional sweating. When you're triggered, your body releases stress hormones like cortisol and adrenaline. These hormones activate your sweat glands, particularly on your palms, soles, and face. This is different from the sweat you produce when you're physically hot. It's a direct response to psychological threat.

So if you find yourself sweating in situations that aren't physically demanding, or if you wake up drenched in sweat after a nightmare, your body is telling you something. It's saying, "I'm still in danger. I'm still fighting. I'm still trying to survive."

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium plays a role in regulating the nervous system and can help with both temperature regulation and sleep disturbances.

How to start regulating your temperature from the inside

So what do you do when your internal thermostat is broken? You can't just will your nervous system to calm down. You can't tell your body, "Hey, we're safe now, please warm up my hands." It doesn't work that way. But there are things you can do to slowly, gently, patiently retrain your nervous system.

The first step is awareness. Start noticing when your temperature shifts and what's happening around you. Are you in a triggering conversation? Are you about to give a presentation? Are you alone in your apartment at night feeling unsafe? The temperature change is a signal. It's information. Don't ignore it.

The second step is grounding. When you notice your hands getting cold or your face getting hot, bring your attention to your body. Feel your feet on the floor. Feel your back against the chair. Take a slow breath. This doesn't fix the problem immediately, but it starts to send a different signal to your nervous system. It says, "I'm here. I'm present. I'm noticing."

The third step is movement. Gentle, slow, mindful movement can help your nervous system shift out of stuck states. Shaking, stretching, rocking, or even just walking can help release the tension that's contributing to temperature dysregulation. A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link). Using it on your legs and back can help signal safety to your nervous system.

The fourth step is warmth that comes from connection. Social engagement, which Porges emphasizes as key to ventral vagal activation, can actually help regulate your body temperature. When you feel safe with another person, your body relaxes. Blood flows back to your extremities. Your hands warm up. This is why a hug from someone you trust can literally make you feel warmer.

But I know that connection isn't always available. And sometimes the people around you are the source of the dysregulation in the first place. So you have to find other ways to signal safety to your body. Warm baths, heated blankets, warm drinks, gentle pressure on your skin. All of these can help coax your nervous system toward a more regulated state.

Stay with me here. This isn't a quick fix. Your nervous system has been shaped by years, maybe decades, of experience. It's not going to rewire overnight. But every time you notice your temperature and respond with kindness rather than frustration, you're building a new pattern. You're teaching your body that it can be heard. That it can be soothed. That it doesn't have to keep fighting alone.

What to do when nothing seems to work

There will be days when none of this helps. When your hands are still freezing and your heart is still racing and you feel like your body is betraying you. On those days, I want you to remember something.

Your body is not your enemy. It's not broken. It's not defective. It's doing exactly what it learned to do to survive. The temperature dysregulation is a symptom of a nervous system that has been working overtime to keep you alive. It's a sign of how hard your body has been fighting for you.

So on the hard days, don't try to fix it. Don't try to force your temperature to change. Just be with it. Put on a sweater if you're cold. Take off your jacket if you're hot. Drink some water. Breathe. And know that this is part of the healing process.

Peter Levine, the developer of Somatic Experiencing, talks about pendulation. The idea that healing happens in cycles. You move toward the discomfort, then you move away. You feel the cold, then you feel the warmth. Your nervous system is learning, slowly, that it can tolerate these sensations without being overwhelmed.

Wild, right? The very thing that feels so wrong, so uncomfortable, so broken, is actually part of your body's wisdom. It's telling you a story about what you've been through. And the more you listen, the more you can respond with compassion instead of fear.

Your temperature will regulate again. Your hands will warm up. Your body will find its way back to balance. Not because you force it, but because you create the conditions for it to happen naturally. Safety, patience, presence, time. These are the ingredients.

And you're already doing the work. You're reading this. You're trying to understand. You're yourself, even when it's hard. That matters more than you know.
